techniques.evidence_quote: access to large datasets of credentials (username/passwords) that they had developed for themselves (suggesting other breaches), traded for, or acquired through public leaks
They had a script developed through a freelancer on Upwork, which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub.
The adversaries may have had about 13 good log-ins for Uber. They accessed these GitHub accounts intending to find AWS credentials
a script ... which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub. ... "Over a hundred thousand" successful log-ins
the adversary discovered hourly encrypted backups ... the `cold-storage` bucket
they would use simple GitHub searches to surface these credentials
They accessed these GitHub accounts intending to find AWS credentials ... succeeded in finding several AWS credentials

techniques.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
techniques.evidence_quote: loaded them into a tool called S3 Browser
there was an "interesting" object which one of the adversaries proceeded to download ... a database containing 50 million records
the adversary used the "S3 Browser" tool with stolen credentials against the AWS API
extortion was intended by the adversary and perceived as extortion by the response team ... a counter of something in the six figures

attack_chain.description: Attackers amassed a database of over 10 million username/password pairs from prior breaches, trades, and public leaks.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: access to large datasets of credentials ... "over ten million" entries

attack_chain.description: Commissioned a credential-stuffing script on Upwork that tested emails/passwords against GitHub and logged the GitHub organizations each valid account belonged to.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a script developed through a freelancer on Upwork, which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub

evidence_chunks.quote: a script developed through a freelancer on Upwork, which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub
a script developed through a freelancer on Upwork, which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub

attack_chain.description: Ran the script against GitHub, producing over 100,000 valid logins, which were cross-referenced with a 'most-visited websites' list to pick Uber as a high-value target.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: "Over a hundred thousand" successful log-ins were output by the adversary's script

attack_chain.description: Authenticated to approximately 13 Uber engineer GitHub accounts using stuffed credentials; MFA was not enforced.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The adversaries may have had about 13 good log-ins for Uber

evidence_chunks.quote: The adversaries may have had about 13 good log-ins for Uber
The adversaries may have had about 13 good log-ins for Uber

attack_chain.description: Used simple GitHub searches within the compromised private repositories to locate AWS access keys (including the long-lived 'genghis' credential) stored in source code.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they would use simple GitHub searches to surface these credentials

evidence_chunks.quote: they would use simple GitHub searches to surface these credentials
they would use simple GitHub searches to surface these credentials

attack_chain.description: Loaded the recovered AWS access keys into the S3 Browser tool and authenticated to Uber's AWS environment from an 'anonymous' VPN since no IAM sourceIPAddress restriction was in place.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: loaded them into a tool called S3 Browser

attack_chain.description: Enumerated S3 buckets and discovered the 'cold-storage' bucket containing hourly encrypted database backups as well as non-automated (unencrypted) backups.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the adversary discovered hourly encrypted backups ... the `cold-storage` bucket

attack_chain.description: Identified an 'interesting' unencrypted manual backup object (created by an engineer decommissioning a service) among the client-side-encrypted files in the cold-storage bucket.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: an engineer created backups while decommissioning a service, writing them without encryption into the same bucket

evidence_chunks.quote: an engineer created backups while decommissioning a service, writing them without encryption into the same bucket
an engineer created backups while decommissioning a service, writing them without encryption into the same bucket

attack_chain.description: Downloaded the plaintext database backup from S3 containing PII of 57 million riders and 7 million drivers (including 600,000 US driver's license numbers).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: one of the adversaries proceeded to download ... This was a database containing 50 million records

attack_chain.description: On 2016-11-14 the adversary emailed the CSO from ProtonMail claiming a major vulnerability and demanded payment; Uber ultimately paid $100,000 via HackerOne in exchange for a data-deletion promise and NDAs.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Company paid hackers $100,000 to delete info, keep quiet

evidence_chunks.quote: Company paid hackers $100,000 to delete info, keep quiet
Company paid hackers $100,000 to delete info, keep quiet

initial_access_vector: Credential stuffing against GitHub using a purchased credential database yielded ~13 valid Uber engineer logins. AWS access keys (including the unrotated 'genghis' key from 2013) were found in a private Uber GitHub repository and used to access S3.
persistence_mechanism: No explicit persistence mechanism; attackers relied on long-lived static AWS access keys that had not been rotated since 2013 and were not restricted by IAM sourceIPAddress policies.
privilege_escalation: None observed; the leaked 'genghis' AWS credentials already had sufficient S3 permissions to list and read the cold-storage backup bucket.
